source.title: A Putative Multiple-Demand System in the Macaque Brain.

confidence_rationale: Solid macaque fMRI connectivity study delineating a frontoparietal+insular MD homolog. Direct support for distributed multi-site rule architecture and for R1 multi-stream integration (insular cluster = interoceptive). Confidence reduced because the paper measures resting-state connectivity rather than task-driven rule modulation, so the MD-as-rule-substrate inference is via the human MD literature analogy.
failure_signatures: If ARC-062 weak reading commits to a single-stream discriminator (z_world only) and FAILs the SD-054 falsifier, the macaque MD network's insular cluster predicts the missing input stream is interoceptive (z_self / z_harm_a analog) — diagnostic via the input-ablation arm of the Phase 2 experiment.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Putative macaque MD regions, mapped from frontoparietal MD regions defined in humans, were found to be functionally connected under anesthesia. Iterative refinement using cross-validated connectivity identified seven clusters across frontoparietal AND INSULAR cortex closely similar to the human MD counterpart, plus one unexpected cluster in the lateral fissure.
mapping.ree_translation: Two architectural commitments land here. First — the MD network spans frontoparietal AND insular cortex, both in humans and now in macaques. The insular cluster is the interoceptive integration site (Craig 2009; Critchley 2003). For R1, this is direct evidence that biological rule-context modulation includes interoceptive input as a first-class member, not as an afterthought. ARC-062's discriminator should therefore read all three streams (z_world + z_self + z_harm_a) — the multi-stream commitment that Miller & Cohen 2001 endorse abstractly, this paper grounds anatomically. Second — the MD network is *distributed* across multiple frontoparietal sites (lateral PFC, dorsomedial frontal, parietal, insular). For R3, this favours the 'all three sites in parallel' framing over 'one primary site'. ARC-062 weak reading at a single site (option iii score_bias) is a useful Phase 1 simplification, but the V3 architectural-end-state will need to recognise the distributed-substrate reality.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Mitchell et al. measured resting-state connectivity, not task-driven rule-context modulation. The MD-as-rule-substrate inference is via analogy to the human MD literature where task-driven adaptive coding has been demonstrated (e.g. Erez & Duncan 2015 — see sibling entry). The paper anchors the *anatomical* multi-site distribution but does not directly demonstrate that all sites participate in rule-context modulation in the macaque under task.
mapping.source_context: Resting-state fMRI in 35 anaesthetised rhesus macaques; iterative cross-validated connectivity refinement; mapping from human MD coordinates.

confidence_components.notes: Solid methodology, peer-reviewed, n=35. Source quality reduced from highest tier because the data is resting-state (not task-driven) and acquired under anaesthesia. Mapping fidelity reduced because the substrate-MD-network correspondence is by anatomical analogy. Transfer risk moderate: macaque-to-REE substrate mapping is direct for the regions involved.
